reasoning: The header injection claim requires that a CRLF-containing value reach header() via the cache-replay path (lines 41-44). The only variable content in the stored headers array is the Content-Type value, which is derived from page_optimize_get_mime_type() (lines 256-267). That function does a lookup in the hardcoded $page_optimize_types array defined at lines 3-6 — only 'text/css' and 'application/javascript' can ever be returned. The strict in_array() guard at line 162-163 calls page_optimize_status_exit(400) for any file whose extension is not in that two-entry table, so no attacker-supplied extension can produce a novel mime type, let alone one containing CRLF. The Last-Modified and Content-Length headers are composed entirely of safe computed values (gmdate output and an integer). No other header is stored in the meta file. An attacker has no code path to inject a CRLF byte into any value that reaches the stored headers array, making the end-to-end attack chain impossible. The sink (header($header) on cache replay) is real, but the source is fully constrained by a closed whitelist before it ever reaches the cache.
